85: /// Optional type color for [`ByteStream`], which determines type compatibility.
86: #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize, Default)]
87: pub enum ByteStreamType {
88:     /// Compatible with [`Type::Binary`], and should only be converted to binary, even when the
89:     /// desired type is unknown.
90:     Binary,
91:     /// Compatible with [`Type::String`], and should only be converted to string, even when the
92:     /// desired type is unknown.
93:     ///
94:     /// This does not guarantee valid UTF-8 data, but it is conventionally so. Converting to
95:     /// `String` still requires validation of the data.
96:     String,
97:     /// Unknown whether the stream should contain binary or string data. This usually is the result
98:     /// of an external stream, e.g. an external command or file.
99:     #[default]
100:     Unknown,
101: }
102: 
103: impl ByteStreamType {
104:     /// Returns the string that describes the byte stream type - i.e., the same as what `describe`
105:     /// produces. This can be used in type mismatch error messages.
106:     pub fn describe(self) -> &'static str {
107:         match self {
108:             ByteStreamType::Binary => "binary (stream)",
109:             ByteStreamType::String => "string (stream)",
110:             ByteStreamType::Unknown => "byte stream",
111:         }
112:     }
113: 
114:     /// Returns true if the type is `Binary` or `Unknown`
115:     pub fn is_binary_coercible(self) -> bool {
116:         matches!(self, ByteStreamType::Binary | ByteStreamType::Unknown)
117:     }
118: 
119:     /// Returns true if the type is `String` or `Unknown`
120:     pub fn is_string_coercible(self) -> bool {
121:         matches!(self, ByteStreamType::String | ByteStreamType::Unknown)
122:     }
123: }
